name: Davis Mine
short_description: Historic pegmatite mine in the Berkshire uplands offering quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens. Gentle slopes and scenic New England terrain make this a moderate rockhounding destination.
description:
Davis Mine is a historic pegmatite occurrence located in Franklin County's Berkshire region of western Massachusetts. The site sits at 1,368 feet elevation on gentle slopes typical of the area's metamorphic highlands. This locality has yielded quality quartz crystals, feldspars, and mica specimens that reflect the region's Precambrian pegmatite-rich bedrock.

The geological setting here is classic New England: Precambrian metamorphic basement rocks intruded by granitic pegmatites during the Ordovician-Silurian mountain-building events. These pegmatite veins crystallized slowly from granitic melts, allowing large crystals of quartz, potassium feldspar, and muscovite mica to develop. The Davis Mine area represents one of many productive pegmatite bodies in the northern Berkshires that have attracted collectors for over a century.

Visitors should expect mixed woodland and open hillside terrain with moderate elevation gain. The collecting surface typically includes weathered pegmatite exposures, mine dumps, and surrounding talus slopes. Specimen quality varies from pocket to pocket, so systematic searching across different elevations yields best results.

This is an excellent location for rockhounders interested in New England pegmatite mineralogy and the region's metamorphic basement geology. The gentle slopes and accessible terrain make it suitable for families and intermediate collectors, though a good field eye and patience pay dividends here.

Bring sturdy digging tools, a backpack for specimens, and plan for a half-day outing. Late spring through early fall offers the most comfortable collecting conditions in the New England climate.
history: Davis Mine is part of the prolific pegmatite belt of western Massachusetts that formed during Precambrian time when granitic magma intruded metamorphic host rocks. These slow-cooling pegmatites crystallized into the large-crystal mineral assemblages that have attracted collectors and miners since the 19th century, with documented collecting activity at pegmatite sites throughout Franklin County dating back over 150 years.
tips: Search the old mine workings and surrounding talus slopes where weathering has exposed fresh pegmatite surfaces and loose specimens. Focus on quartz-rich pockets where feldspar and mica often cluster together; look for glassy quartz points and pearlescent feldspar cleavage faces. A small pry bar or cold chisel works well for carefully extracting specimens from the pegmatite matrix without shattering them. Bring a geology field guide to identify feldspar varieties (orthoclase vs. plagioclase) and distinguish muscovite mica from biotite.
terrain_notes: Elevation 1,368 feet on gentle slopes with mixed woodland and scrub vegetation. Terrain is moderately uneven with exposed bedrock, talus, and old mine workings. Walking distance from parking varies depending on access point; expect 0.5 to 2 miles of hiking over rocky but not steep ground. Footing is generally stable but requires sturdy boots due to loose rock and uneven pegmatite blocks.
best_season: May - October. Spring through fall offers comfortable temperatures and good trail conditions. Winter snowfall and mud make access challenging; summer heat is moderate at this elevation but insects are active in June-July.
written_directions: From Rowe town center, take Route 2 west approximately 1.5 miles toward the Mohawk Trail. Look for Forest Road or local access roads branching north toward the Berkshire uplands. The exact mine access may require local knowledge or parking along Route 2 with hiking into the pegmatite belt. GPS coordinates 42.683431, -72.864959 should be entered into a mapping application for final navigation.
road_conditions: Route 2 is paved and well-maintained. Final access to mine workings requires hiking; old logging roads or trails may be rough or overgrown.
parking_notes: Limited roadside parking along Route 2 or at informal pullouts near forest access roads. No developed parking lot; expect to find a safe spot off-road with scenic views.
collection_rules: Personal rockhounding use only. Collect specimens for your collection, not for resale or commercial purposes. Respect private property boundaries if present; verify access before collecting. Stay clear of unstable mine workings or old shafts. Leave the site in good condition and do not excavate large areas.
rules:
Personal use collection only
No commercial resale of specimens
Avoid unstable mine structures
Respect property boundaries
Small hand tool collection only
nearest_services: Rowe (small village) approximately 1.5 miles south; Shelburne Falls (larger town) approximately 8 miles southeast with gas, food, and lodging.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven rocky footing and talus slopes. No wheelchair access due to hiking requirement and rough ground. Sturdy footwear and moderate fitness level recommended. Older adults or those with limited mobility may find the 0.5-2 mile walk and rocky terrain challenging but achievable at own pace.

faq.question: What minerals can you find at Davis Mine in Massachusetts?
faq.answer: Davis Mine is known for quartz crystals, feldspars (primarily potassium feldspar), and muscovite mica. These minerals are typical of the Precambrian pegmatites found throughout the northern Berkshires. Crystal sizes and quality vary, but the location has historically produced nice display specimens.

faq.question: How much does it cost to rockhound at Davis Mine in Rowe?
faq.answer: Davis Mine is a public rockhounding location with no entrance fee or permit requirement for personal use collection. However, access may require parking near Route 2 and hiking into the pegmatite belt, so plan accordingly for time and gas costs.

faq.question: Is Davis Mine beginner-friendly for rockhounding?
faq.answer: Yes, Davis Mine is suitable for beginners and families with children age 8 and up. The gentle slopes and accessible pegmatite exposures make it easier than many New England mining sites, though moderate hiking fitness is required. Bring basic hand tools and a field guide to identify your finds.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Davis Mine?
faq.answer: The best collecting season is May through October. Spring and fall offer comfortable temperatures, stable ground, and good visibility of pegmatite exposures. Winter snow and mud make access difficult; summer is warm but insect activity peaks in June-July.

faq.question: What tools should I bring to collect at Davis Mine in Massachusetts?
faq.answer: Bring a small pry bar, cold chisel, hammer, and sturdy digging tools sized for hand collection. A backpack, field guide to minerals, and small cloth bags for specimens are essential. Gloves protect hands when handling rough pegmatite blocks.

faq.question: Can you legally collect minerals at Davis Mine?
faq.answer: Yes, personal rockhounding and specimen collection are allowed at Davis Mine for non-commercial use. Commercial resale of specimens is not permitted. Respect any private property boundaries and avoid unstable mine shafts or old workings.

meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and mica at Davis Mine near Rowe, MA. Public pegmatite rockhounding site with moderate hiking on gentle slopes.
